\begin{document}

\title{Defense-in-Depth Runtime Safety in Move}
\titlerunning{Defense-in-Depth Runtime Safety in Move}

\author{Victor Gao \and
        Wolfgang Grieskamp \and
        Vineeth Kashyap \and
        George Mitenkov \and \\
        Teng Zhang \and
        Runtian Zhou \and
        Andrea Cappa \and
        Marco Illadi}
\authorrunning{V. Gao et al.}
\institute{Aptos Labs, Palo Alto, USA}

\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Move is a smart-contract language used to execute transactions on the Aptos blockchain.
Move programs execute in a sandboxed VM as typed bytecode.
The VM statically verifies a few foundational safety properties --- in particular type safety and reference safety --- ahead of execution, during code loading time.
In principle, this design gives strong guarantees for Move.
However, the static verification logic is complex and continually evolving with changes in the language.
Just like any other software, the static verifier is not immune to bugs: historically, there have been subtle errors in the verifier that can let rule-violating bytecode through.
In a live blockchain setting, such bugs are not academic: a missed type or reference rule violation can translate directly into loss of assets, forged authority, or unrecoverable corruption of on-chain state.
For this reason, Aptos relies on defense in depth, in the form of \emph{runtime safety checks} that independently verify the critical invariants during execution, providing protection against both compromised bytecode and latent verifier bugs.
This paper describes the runtime safety checks in place for Move on Aptos.
\keywords{Move \and Smart contracts \and Runtime verification \and Type safety \and Reference safety.}
\end{abstract}
